messages.content: Answer the Call of the Wilds on October 3 for a week-long celebration of our first expansion, the Rise of the Angry Earth.  - rephrase this
Join us on October 3rd for a week-long celebration as we proudly embrace the Call of the Wilds, commemorating the launch of our first expansion, the Rise of the Angry Earth.

Whether you tune in to watch your favorite personality or to learn more about a new feature that interests you, this selection of Twitch streamers offers something for every type of player. - rephrase this

No matter your preference, whether it be watching your beloved streamer or exploring exciting new features, this curated collection of Twitch streamers guarantees entertainment and information catered to every type of player.
Look forward to tips, insight, and memorable moments during the following segments: - rephrase this
Anticipate valuable tips, illuminating insights, and unforgettable moments to arise during the upcoming segments:
Check out the Call of the Wilds for unique Twitch Drops like a Flail skin and custom Mount Flag Attachment. -  rephrase this
Don't miss out on the exclusive Twitch Drops available during the Call of the Wilds event, including a one-of-a-kind Flail skin and a customized Mount Flag Attachment. Be sure to check it out!

Nicolaus Copernicus belonged to the clerical state, but he did not hold higher (priestly) orders. He took lower orders to become a canon of Warmia, but it is not known when this happened. It was probably before 8 November 1495, when his name appeared in one of the lists of chapter members. As many note, he never aspired to the priesthood, considering himself first and foremost a scientist. Copernicus lived at a time of the vigorous growth of the Reformation. However, unlike many other priests, including the Warmian canons, he remained faithful to the Catholic Church, and dedicated his work De revolutionibus to Pope Paul III. Nicolaus Copernicus wrote: "When the Lateran Council discussed the question of improving the Church calendar, it was left unresolved only because sufficiently precise measurements of years and months, as well as of the movements of the Sun and Moon, were not yet available. Since then [...] I have begun to strain my mind to examine these things more closely". - rephrase this

Nicolaus Copernicus was a member of the clerical state but did not hold higher priestly orders. Instead, he took lower orders to become a canon of Warmia. The exact date of this occurrence is unknown, but it is believed to have transpired prior to November 8, 1495, evident from his appearance on a list of chapter members. Copernicus, primarily considering himself a scientist, never aspired to the priesthood. Despite living in a time of significant Reformation movement, he remained steadfastly loyal to the Catholic Church, even dedicating his work, De revolutionibus, to Pope Paul III. Copernicus acknowledged the unresolved issue of improving the Church calendar during the Lateran Council, attributing the lack of precision in measurements of years, months, and the movements of celestial bodies at that time. However, he personally undertook the task of examining these matters more closely.

21 April 1506 - Lidzbark. Łukasz Watzenrode, bishop of Warmia (and Nicolaus Copernicus), accompanied by Ambroży Pampowski, starost of Malbork, leave Lidzbark on their way to the royal court in Vilnius - rephrase this

On April 21, 1506, in Lidzbark, Łukasz Watzenrode, the bishop of Warmia, along with Nicolaus Copernicus, set out on a journey to the royal court in Vilnius. They were accompanied by Ambroży Pampowski, the starost of Malbork.

Despite his wide-ranging interests, Nicolaus Copernicus obtained only the academic title of Doctor of Laws. As a lawyer, he was to assist and advise his uncle, Bishop Lukas Watzenrode. He travelled with him to sejms, conventions of states and on numerous journeys. He spent the years from 1503 to 1509 with his uncle, who probably wanted to make him his successor. Among his contemporaries, Copernicus was highly regarded as a lawyer and advisor, but more valuable and, at the time, a rare physician. It was this latter skill that made him a frequent guest at the bishops' courts. He treated both his uncle and his successors. - rephrase this

Although Nicolaus Copernicus had a broad range of interests, he only obtained the academic title of Doctor of Laws. His role as a lawyer was to provide assistance and guidance to his uncle, Bishop Lukas Watzenrode. Copernicus frequently accompanied his uncle to sejms (state conventions) and on various journeys. From 1503 to 1509, he spent considerable time with his uncle, who possibly intended to groom him as his successor. Among his peers, Copernicus enjoyed a high reputation as a lawyer and advisor, but his expertise as a physician was even more valued and relatively rare during that era. This medical skill often led him to be a welcomed guest at the courts of bishops, where he had the opportunity to treat not only his uncle but also his successors.

5 January 1520 - Braniewo. Archdeacon Jan Sculteti and Nicolaus Copernicus, canons of Warmia, as envoys of the Warmian bishop Fabian Luzjański to the great master Albrecht in the city of Braniewo occupied by him. - rephrase this

On January 5, 1520, in Braniewo, Archdeacon Jan Sculteti and Nicolaus Copernicus, both canons of Warmia, were dispatched as envoys by Warmian bishop Fabian Luzjański to meet with the great master Albrecht in the occupied city of Braniewo.

As a canon of Warmia, Nicolaus Copernicus performed a number of administrative functions caring for the property and estates belonging to the Chapter. He was an administrator in Olsztyn (1516-19; 1520-21), placed landlords on empty fields, secured the town and castle against attack from the Teutonic Knights during the war which ended with the Prussian Homage in 1525. Olsztyn, in turn, was an important town for the Chapter at that time. Its treasury was moved here from Frombork.
Staying in Warmia, Copernicus, apart from his professional activity as a lawyer and doctor, devoted himself to continuing his astronomical research. He used moments free from other duties to do so. In Olsztyn in 1517, while living in the castle, he researched the issue of the equinoxes, as evidenced by a surviving memento - a plaque on the cloister. - rephrase this

While serving as a canon of Warmia, Nicolaus Copernicus took on various administrative responsibilities, particularly overseeing the management of the Chapter's properties and estates. He held the position of administrator in Olsztyn during different periods, from 1516-1519 and again from 1520-1521. During his tenure, he allocated landlords to vacant fields and played a pivotal role in fortifying the town and castle against potential attacks from the Teutonic Knights during the conflict that culminated in the Prussian Homage in 1525. Olsztyn held significant importance for the Chapter during that time, leading to the relocation of its treasury from Frombork to Olsztyn.

Despite his administrative duties, Copernicus remained committed to his astronomical research, utilizing any available free time to advance his studies. While residing in the castle of Olsztyn in 1517, he delved into the topic of equinoxes, which is evidenced by a preserved memento—a plaque on the cloister.

Nicolaus Copernicus died at the age of 70, a very ripe age in the 16th century. The exact date of his death is not known. His friend, the later Bishop of Warmia, Tidemann Giese, gave the date as 24 May 1543, while some researchers now also favour a slightly earlier date of 21 May. - rephrase this

Nicolaus Copernicus passed away at the remarkable age of 70, a significant lifespan during the 16th century. The precise date of his death remains uncertain. According to his friend Tidemann Giese, who would later become the Bishop of Warmia, Copernicus's demise occurred on May 24, 1543. However, some contemporary researchers now lean towards an alternative date, slightly earlier on May 21.
Nicolaus Copernicus was buried in Frombork Cathedral, but the exact location was soon forgotten. Today, epitaphs can be found in two different naves. - rephrase this

After his passing, Nicolaus Copernicus was laid to rest in Frombork Cathedral; however, with the passage of time, the precise burial site was eventually forgotten. Presently, two separate naves within the cathedral house epitaphs dedicated to him.
